data.content_ref: inline:{"analogy":{"adopted_commitment_refs":[],"disanalogies":["Direct manipulation is typically single-user; this is multi-operator via MCP","Linearizability is about concurrent operations; this is about temporal feedback","Session guarantees are usually weaker consistency; here the system is correct but unresponsive"],"overturn_conditions":["The attention fence is proven to complete in bounded time that is acceptable for interactive use","A user study shows operators prefer batch-visible plans to incremental feedback","The scratch authoring already supports streaming commit with deterministic replay reconstruction"],"shared_structure":["A system provides strong consistency guarantees (deterministic replay/linearizability)","Users expect responsive, interruptible interaction (direct manipulation/session guarantees)","The strong consistency mechanism requires batching or fencing that delays feedback","The user's mental model includes intermediate states that the system does not record","Historical analysis cannot reconstruct user intent from system state alone"],"source_memory_refs":["hci-research: gulf-of-evaluation-in-direct-manipulation","distributed-systems: linearizability-vs-session-guarantees"],"transfer_claims":["The 'gulf of evaluation' pattern: the attention fence widens the gulf between operator intent and system state; mitigation requires either exposing fence internals (violating determinism) or adding a parallel operator-intent stream (violating single-source-of-truth)","The 'session linearizability' pattern: a separate 'operator session' abstraction with weaker guarantees can overlay the strong harness replay, with explicit merge/conflict semantics at fence points","The 'progressive disclosure' pattern: CLI/MCP/chat surfaces receive scratch blocks as they complete, with attention coverage receipts updated optimistically and corrected at fence, rather than frozen at fence"]},"claim":"The attention/coverage system (src/deepreason/scratch/attention.py:405-469) and scratch authoring (src/deepreason/scratch/authoring.py:27-119) implement a deterministic fence that is formally correct for replay but creates an operator-experience failure mode in CLI/MCP/chat contexts where real-time feedback and intervention are expected, violating C13 (shared services) and C14 (historical intelligibility) by making scratch attention appear 'frozen' to operators while the harness continues processing.","counterconditions":[{"case":"CLI/MCP/chat operators do not need real-time feedback; batch-mode replay is acceptable","eval":"observation"},{"case":"The attention fence includes intermediate checkpoints that operators can inspect","eval":"observation"},{"case":"Scratch authoring emits streaming events that are committed incrementally, not at task boundary","eval":"observation"},{"case":"A separate 'operator intent' log exists that captures attention redirections outside the formal harness","eval":"observation"}],"definitions":[],"derivation":[],"formalization_refs":[],"mechanism":"Attention selection and coverage receipts are deterministically planned and appended at a state fence (attention.py:405-469). Scratch authoring offers one bounded model task over a committed advisory context and persists typed non-authoritative blocks (authoring.py:27-119). The combination: attention is planned in advance at a fence, then scratch authoring executes within that plan. For replay, this is correct: the same plan, same execution. For operator experience in CLI/MCP/chat: the operator sees a plan committed to the ledger before any model output, then must wait for the bounded task to complete with no visibility into progress, no ability to redirect attention based on intermediate results, and no way to distinguish 'harness is still working' from 'model is stuck' from 'attention plan was poor'. This is not a correctness failure (C3 canonical replay works) but a UX-architecture mismatch: the deterministic fence that enables replay prevents the incremental, interruptible, redirectable interaction that CLI/MCP/chat surfaces require. The historical intelligibility failure (C14) is that future operators replaying a session see the attention plan as 'what was intended' and the scratch blocks as 'what was produced', but cannot reconstruct 'what the operator knew when' or 'what alternatives were visible', because the fence eliminates intermediate epistemic states from the record.","premises":[],"schema":"deepreason-reasoning-envelope-v1","scope":{"covers":[],"excludes":[]},"simulation_refs":[],"uncertainties":[]}
